---
title: "2.4 Nested if Statements"
subtitle: "Part 2: Debugging and Dangling Else"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit2-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Debug Task

### `activecode:: ifElseIfDebug`

Textbook prompt: The else-if connection is necessary if you want to hook up conditionals together. In the following code, there are 4 separate if statements instead of the if-else-if pattern.

Will this code print out the correct grade? First, trace through the code to see why it prints out the incorrect grade. Then, fix the code by adding in 3 `else` keywords to connect the if statements and see if it works.

## Code Window

### `activecode:: ifElseIfDebug`

::: {.code-scroll .compact}
```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class IfDebug
{
   public static void main(String[] args)
   {
       Scanner scan = new Scanner(System.in);
       int score = scan.nextInt();
       String grade = "";

       if (score >= 90)
       {
           grade = "A";
       }
       if (score >= 80)
       {
           grade = "B";
       }
       if (score >= 70)
       {
           grade = "C";
       }
       if (score >= 60)
       {
           grade = "D";
       }
       else
       {
           grade = "F";
       }

       System.out.println(grade);
   }
}
```
:::

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: ifElseIfDebug`

Runestone checks the fixed program with these inputs:

| input score | expected grade |
|---|---|
| `95` | `A` |
| `85` | `B` |
| `75` | `C` |
| `65` | `D` |
| `55` | `F` |

## Battery Task

### `activecode:: ifelseifBattery`

Textbook prompt: Finish the following code so that it prints `"Plug in your phone!"` if the battery is below `50`, `"Unplug your phone!"` if it is above `100`, and `"All okay!"` otherwise.

Change the battery value to test all 3 conditions.

## Code Window

### `activecode:: ifelseifBattery`

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class BatteryTest
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        Scanner scan = new Scanner(System.in);
        int battery = scan.nextInt();

        System.out.println("All okay!");
    }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: ifelseifBattery`

Runestone checks these inputs:

| input battery | expected output |
|---|---|
| `95` | `All okay!` |
| `100` | `Unplug your phone!` |
| `45` | `Plug in your phone!` |

The source prompt says "above 100"; the Runestone test boundary uses `100`.

## Dangling Else Statements

### Closest unmatched if

Sometimes with nested ifs we find a <span class="term">dangling else</span> that could potentially belong to either if statement.

The rule is that the else clause will always be a part of the <span class="mark">closest unmatched if statement</span> in the same block of code, regardless of indentation.

```java
// Nested if with dangling else
if (boolean expression)
   if (boolean expression)
      Do statement;
   else  // belongs to closest if
      Do other statement;
```

## Debug Task

### `activecode:: danglingelse`

Textbook prompt: Try the following code with a dangling else. Notice that the indentation does not matter to the compiler. How could you get the else to belong to the first if statement?

## Code Window

### `activecode:: danglingelse`

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class DanglingElseTest
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
       Scanner in = new Scanner(System.in);

       boolean sunny = in.nextBoolean();
       boolean hot = in.nextBoolean();

       if (sunny)
           if (hot)
              System.out.println("Head for the beach!");
         else // Which if is else attached to??
           System.out.println("Bring your umbrella!");
   }
}
```
:::

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: danglingelse`

Runestone checks the fixed program with these inputs:

| input | expected output |
|---|---|
| `true false` | empty output |
| `true true` | `Head for the beach!` |
| `false false` | `Bring your umbrella!` |
| `false true` | `Bring your umbrella!` |

## Braces Fix

### Make else belong to the top-level if

You can use curly braces `{}` to enclose a nested if and have the else clause belong to the top level if clause like below:

```java
// Nested if with dangling else
if (boolean expression)
{
   if (boolean expression)
      Do this statement;
}
else  // belongs to first if
  Do that statement;
```

Many experienced Java programmers always use curly braces, even when they are not technically required to avoid this kind of confusion.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- explain why separate if statements can overwrite earlier grade assignments
- connect mutually exclusive grade ranges with `else if`
- preserve the battery prompt and test boundary when writing conditions
- state that `else` attaches to the closest unmatched `if`
- use braces to force an else to belong to the top-level if
- test dangling-else code with all four Boolean input pairs
:::

## End

### Continue to Part 3

[Next: Adventure challenge →](2-4-part-3-adventure-challenge.html)
